frameworks.name: maternal immune activation
frameworks.one_line: Gestational cytokine exposure as a perturbation to offspring neurodevelopment.

frameworks.name: novelty preference / visual paired comparison
frameworks.one_line: Looking-time asymmetry between novel and familiar stimuli as an attention/recognition readout.

frameworks.name: neonatal imitation
frameworks.one_line: Species-typical imitation of social signals as an early developmental milestone.

core_question: Does maternal immune activation produce detectable disruptions in attentional processing during infancy in a nonhuman primate model?
key_assumptions: Rhesus macaques offer translational validity for human neurodevelopment.
Looking time in VPC indexes attention and stimulus processing.
Imitation at 1 week reflects species-typical social-signal processing.
manipulations: MIA-exposed vs control offspring (gestational immune challenge).
Stimulus novelty (novel vs familiar) in VPC.
Stimulus class (abstract images at 1 month; monkey faces at 3–4 months).
Discrimination difficulty (easy vs harder novel-vs-familiar contrasts).
key_findings: At 1 week, MIA offspring deviated from species-typical imitation of human social signals.
At 1 month, MIA offspring looked longer at novel abstract stimuli than controls.
At 3–4 months, MIA offspring looked longer at novel monkey faces than controls.
MIA group's prolonged novelty looking was contingent on easy discriminations.
interpretations: Early attentional processes are disrupted in MIA-exposed infants, likely yielding longer visual processing times.
Infant NHP attention markers may translate to identifying children affected by gestational infection.
limitations_noted: Few prior NHP MIA studies have probed infancy, limiting comparison.
